sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Matthew, presents with a chief complaint of back pain that has been ongoing for a while, with a reported duration of about a month.
He describes the pain as an electrical toothache-like sensation that radiates from his lower back down to his left leg, specifically to the big toe.
He rates his pain as a 6 out of 10, but it can reach up to 13 out of 10 when it worsens.
The pain is exacerbated by standing, walking, coughing, and sneezing, but is somewhat relieved by lying down.
He also reports weakness in both legs and numbness in the calf.
He has been unable to perform daily activities, including tying his shoes, and has been bedridden at times.
Past medical history: The patient reports a history of a fall from a roof 30 years ago, which may have resulted in a fracture or disk injury.
He also mentions that he has been unable to play football due to his back pain.
Review of systems: The patient reports no numbness or tingling in his lower extremities, except for the calf area.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vitals are not mentioned in the transcript, so I will leave this section blank.
Physical exam: On examination, the patient is positive for pain to palpation at the L4 vertebrae, and also positive for pain with flexion and extension of the back.
He has 4 out of 5 strength in the left leg and 5 out of 5 strength in the right leg.
Reflexes are brisk and motor and sensory are intact in both lower extremities.
Test Results: The MRI shows a disk herniation at the level of L4-L5 vertebrae with associated nerve root impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diagnosis of acute disk herniation at L4-L5 with nerve root impingement, causing lower back pain and leg pain.
Problem list: Back pain; leg pain; disk herniation at L4-L5 vertebrae; nerve root impingement; weakness in both legs.
Plan:
I recommended an epidural steroid injection to be performed today, which involves placing anti-inflammatory medication at the spot of inflammation.
I also referred the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the area.
The patient is expected to see me again in about a month to assess the effectiveness of the injection.
Follow-up: The patient will see me again in about a month to assess the effectiveness of the epidural steroid injection and to review further treatment plans.
